questions.question: Derive the universal law of gravitation and explain its significance.
questions.answer: The universal law of gravitation states that every object attracts every other object with a force proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. The formula is F = G(Mm)/r². This law explains planetary motion and the falling of objects.

questions.question: Explain how gravitational force is responsible for planetary motion.
questions.answer: Gravitational attraction between the Sun and planets keeps the planets moving in elliptical orbits around the Sun.

questions.question: Explain the derivation and significance of acceleration due to gravity.
questions.answer: Acceleration due to gravity is derived from Newton's law as g = GM/R². It represents the acceleration experienced by objects due to Earth's gravity.
answer_outline: Dependence on Earth mass and radius

questions.question: Explain why the value of g varies with altitude and depth.
questions.answer: The value of g decreases with altitude because distance from Earth's center increases. It also decreases with depth because the mass contributing to gravitational pull decreases.

questions.question: Compare mass and weight and explain their relationship mathematically.
questions.answer: Mass is the amount of matter in an object and remains constant. Weight is the gravitational force acting on it and is calculated by W = mg.

questions.question: Explain how pressure depends on force and area with examples.
questions.answer: Pressure is defined as force per unit area. Increasing force increases pressure while increasing area decreases pressure.

questions.question: Explain why buildings have wide foundations using pressure concept.
questions.answer: Wide foundations increase surface area and reduce pressure on the ground ensuring stability.

questions.question: Explain buoyant force and derive its relation with displaced fluid.
questions.answer: Buoyant force is the upward force exerted by a fluid. According to Archimedes' principle it equals the weight of the displaced fluid.

questions.question: Explain the principle of flotation.
questions.answer: A floating body displaces its own weight of fluid. When buoyant force equals weight, the body floats.

questions.question: Explain Archimedes' principle with experimental verification.
questions.answer: Archimedes' principle states that a body immersed in a fluid experiences an upward force equal to the weight of the displaced fluid. This can be verified using a spring balance and water container.

questions.question: Explain why a steel ship floats but a small steel nail sinks.
questions.answer: Ships displace large volumes of water creating enough buoyant force while nails displace very little water.

questions.question: Explain the working principle of hydrometers.
questions.answer: Hydrometers measure liquid density based on Archimedes' principle by observing the level at which the instrument floats.

questions.question: Explain why submarines can float, sink and remain suspended.
questions.answer: Submarines adjust ballast tanks to change density allowing control over buoyancy.

questions.question: Explain the importance of gravity in maintaining Earth's atmosphere.
questions.answer: Earth's gravitational force holds atmospheric gases close to the planet preventing them from escaping into space.

questions.question: Explain why tides occur in oceans.
questions.answer: Tides occur due to gravitational attraction between Earth, Moon and Sun which pulls ocean water.

questions.question: Explain the concept of apparent weight in fluids.
questions.answer: Apparent weight equals actual weight minus buoyant force.

questions.question: Explain the role of density in buoyancy.
questions.answer: The buoyant force depends on fluid density and volume of displaced fluid.

questions.question: Explain why g is slightly higher at the poles than at the equator.
questions.answer: Earth is flattened at poles so distance from center is smaller resulting in higher gravity.

questions.question: Explain why pressure in liquids increases with depth.
questions.answer: Pressure increases with depth because the weight of liquid above increases.

questions.question: Explain the applications of Archimedes' principle in daily life.
questions.answer: Applications include ship design, hydrometers, submarines and lactometers.

questions.question: Explain gravitational force as a universal force.
questions.answer: Gravitational force acts between all masses in the universe including planets, stars and objects on Earth.

questions.question: Explain why astronauts experience weightlessness in orbit.
questions.answer: Astronauts are in continuous free fall around Earth producing weightlessness.

questions.question: Explain the relationship between buoyancy and floating stability of ships.
questions.answer: Floating stability depends on buoyant force balancing weight and proper distribution of mass.

questions.question: Explain the importance of gravitational force in the universe.
questions.answer: Gravity controls planetary motion, galaxy formation and keeps celestial bodies bound together.

questions.question: Explain how Archimedes' principle is applied in measuring density.
questions.answer: Density can be measured by determining the weight of fluid displaced by an object immersed in it.
